title: Economic Concepts for Financial Planners
description: Explain the business cycle and its phases, monetary and fiscal policy, and key economic indicators

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding economic fundamentals helps planners anticipate market conditions and advise clients appropriately. Economic concepts inform asset allocation decisions, interest rate expectations, and inflation protection strategies.

sections.title: The Business Cycle
sections.content: The economy moves through predictable phases. Understanding where we are in the cycle helps inform investment and planning decisions.

sections.title: Four Phases of the Business Cycle
Key Business Cycle Definitions

content.definition: Two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth (informal definition)
Severe, prolonged recession (>10% GDP decline or >3 years)
Period from trough to previous peak

items: **Expansion**: GDP rising, unemployment falling, consumer confidence high.
**Peak**: The height of economic activity; capacity constraints appear; inflation risk.
**Contraction (Recession)**: GDP falling (2 quarters), unemployment rising.
**Trough**: The bottom of the cycle; point where contraction ends and expansion begins.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Business Cycle Phases
sections.content: **E**very **P**olice **C**aptain **T**akes Patrol → **E**xpansion, **P**eak, **C**ontraction, **T**rough

sections.title: Monetary Policy - The Federal Reserve
sections.content: The **Federal Reserve (Fed)** controls money supply and interest rates. Understanding Fed actions is critical for anticipating interest rate changes and market reactions.

sections.title: Fed's Dual Mandate
sections.content:
The Fed has two primary objectives:

1. **Maximum employment** (full employment)
2. **Price stability** (low, stable inflation - target 2%)

sections.title: Federal Reserve Policy Tools
rows: Rate banks charge each other overnight
Rate Fed charges banks for loans

sections.title: Fiscal Policy - Congress & President
sections.content: **Fiscal policy** involves government spending and taxation, controlled by elected officials rather than the Fed.

sections.title: Fiscal Policy Types

content.definition: Increase government spending, reduce taxes. Goal: Stimulate economic growth
Decrease government spending, increase taxes. Goal: Cool overheating economy, reduce deficits

items: **Expansionary**: Lower taxes / Increase gov spending (Fight recession).
**Contractionary**: Raise taxes / Decrease gov spending (Fight inflation).
**Neutral**: Policy tries to maintain steady growth.

sections.title: Key Economic Indicators
sections.content: Economic indicators fall into three categories based on their timing relative to economic activity.

sections.title: Inflation and Interest Rates
sections.content: Inflation erodes purchasing power over time. Understanding inflation types and measures is essential for planning real (after-inflation) returns.

sections.title: Types of Inflation
rows: Too much money chasing too few goods
Increased production costs passed to consumers
Rising wages increase costs and prices

sections.title: Inflation Measures
rows: Consumer Price Index - urban consumer basket

sections.title: The Yield Curve
sections.content: The yield curve shows the relationship between interest rates and bond maturities. Its shape has significant predictive value.

sections.title: Yield Curve Shapes
rows: Long rates > short rates; healthy economy expected
Short rates > long rates; recession predictor

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Alert: Inverted Yield Curve
sections.content: An **inverted yield curve** is a historically reliable recession predictor. Know this for the exam: when short-term rates exceed long-term rates, it often signals economic contraction ahead.

sections.title: Economic Concepts Applied to Planning
sections.content: Understanding economic phases helps planners make asset allocation recommendations and set client expectations.

sections.title: 📊 Real Return Formulas
sections.content:
**Approximate Method:**
$$\text{Real Return} = \text{Nominal Return} - \text{Inflation}$$

**Exact Method:**
$$\text{Real Return} = \frac{1 + \text{Nominal}}{1 + \text{Inflation}} - 1$$

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Business cycle: Expansion → Peak → Contraction → Trough → repeat
Fed controls monetary policy through interest rates and money supply
Leading indicators predict; coincident measure; lagging confirm
Inverted yield curve historically predicts recessions
Rising rates: Bad for bonds, mixed for stocks, good for savers
Inflation erodes purchasing power - plan with real returns

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST describes an economy in the RECOVERY phase of the business cycle?
options: Unemployment is high, consumer spending is low, and businesses are hesitant to invest.
GDP is declining for two consecutive quarters, and the stock market is experiencing significant losses.
GDP is increasing, unemployment is decreasing, and consumer confidence is rising.
Inflation is rapidly increasing, interest rates are rising, and the economy is overheating.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The recovery phase is characterized by increasing GDP, decreasing unemployment, and rising consumer confidence as the economy begins to bounce back from a recession. The other options describe recessionary or overheating conditions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens *after* a recession.

reveal.question: What is the commonly accepted definition of a recession?
reveal.answer: A significant decline in economic activity spread across the economy, lasting more than a few months, normally visible in real GDP, real income, employment, industrial production, and wholesale-retail sales. A common shorthand definition is two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth.

rows.label: Duration
values: Typically lasts a few months to a year or two

rows.label: Impact
values: Increased unemployment, decreased consumer spending and investment
Massive unemployment, business failures, and widespread poverty

title: Consumer Debt Management Strategies
description: Distinguish between debt payoff strategies and apply credit score factors to financial planning

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Effective debt management is fundamental to financial health and a common focus of financial planning engagements. Helping clients understand and prioritize debt repayment can dramatically improve their financial outcomes.

sections.title: Types of Consumer Debt
sections.content: Consumer debt falls into two major categories based on whether collateral is required.

sections.title: Secured Debt
sections.content: Secured debt is backed by collateral that can be repossessed if payments aren't made.

sections.title: Unsecured Debt
sections.content: Unsecured debt is not backed by collateral, resulting in higher rates due to increased lender risk.

sections.title: Debt Payoff Strategies
sections.content: Two primary strategies exist for prioritizing debt payoff. Both work - the right choice depends on client psychology.

sections.title: 1. Debt Avalanche (Highest Interest First)
sections.content: **Method**: Pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward **highest rate** debt first.

sections.title: 2. Debt Snowball (Smallest Balance First)
sections.content: **Method**: Pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward **smallest balance** debt first.

sections.title: 📊 Strategy Comparison Example
sections.content:
**Client has:**
- Card A: $5,000 at 22%
- Card B: $2,000 at 15%
- Card C: $8,000 at 18%

**Extra payment:** $300/month above minimums

| Strategy | Order | Total Interest |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| **Avalanche** | A → C → B | Lowest |
| **Snowball** | B → A → C | Higher, but faster wins |

**Planner's Role:** Both work. Choose based on client personality. Some need quick wins (snowball); others optimize math (avalanche).

sections.title: The Cost of Minimum Payments
sections.content: Minimum payments can dramatically extend payoff time and multiply total interest paid.

sections.title: 📊 Credit Card Minimum Payment Example
sections.content:
**Balance:** $10,000
**Rate:** 20% APR
**Minimum payment:** 2% of balance (at least $25)

| Payment Strategy | Time to Payoff | Total Interest |
|------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Minimum only | 30+ years | $19,000+ |
| $300/month fixed | 44 months | $3,137 |
| $500/month fixed | 24 months | $1,759 |

sections.title: ⚠️ Minimum Payment Warning
sections.content: Minimum payments can result in paying **twice the original balance** in interest! Always show clients the true cost of minimum-only payments.

sections.title: Balance Transfer Strategies
sections.content: Balance transfers move high-rate debt to a new card with 0% intro APR (typically 12-21 months).

sections.title: Balance Transfer Considerations
rows: New account may temporarily lower score

sections.title: 📊 Balance Transfer Break-Even Analysis
sections.content:
**Transfer $10,000 at 20% APR → 0% card with 3% fee**

**Monthly interest saved:** $10,000 × 20%/12 = $167/month
**Transfer fee:** $10,000 × 3% = $300

**Break-even:** $300 / $167 = **1.8 months**

If paying off within intro period, transfer saves money.

sections.title: Debt Consolidation Options
sections.content: Consolidation combines multiple debts into a single payment, potentially at a lower rate.

sections.title: ⚠️ Home Equity Warning
sections.content: Converting unsecured debt to secured debt (using home equity) risks losing the home if payments aren't made. Use this strategy cautiously.

sections.title: Credit Scores and Financial Planning
sections.content: FICO scores range from 300-850 and affect loan rates, insurance premiums, and rental applications.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FICO Factors
sections.content: **35-30-15-10-10**: Payment history is most important (35%), followed by utilization (30%). Together they're 65% of your score!

sections.title: Good Debt vs. Bad Debt
sections.content: While the "good debt vs. bad debt" framework is useful, remember that any debt becomes "bad" if it's unaffordable or prevents achieving financial goals.

sections.title: Generally "Good" Debt
items: **Mortgage**: Interest is deductible (usually), asset (home) generally appreciates.
**Student Loans**: Investment in human capital; potentially deductible interest.
**Business Debt**: Leverage to generate income; interest deductible.

sections.title: Generally "Bad" Debt
items: **Credit Cards**: High interest, non-deductible, consumption-based.
**Auto Loans**: Asset depreciates rapidly (unless business use).
**Payday Loans**: Predatory interest rates.

sections.title: 📊 Key Formulas
sections.content:
**Monthly Interest:**
$$\text{Monthly Interest} = \text{Balance} \times \frac{\text{APR}}{12}$$

**Transfer Break-Even:**
$$\text{Break-even Months} = \frac{\text{Transfer Fee}}{\text{Monthly Interest Saved}}$$

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Debt avalanche (highest rate) saves most money; snowball (smallest balance) provides quick wins
Minimum payments can result in paying 2× the original balance in interest
Balance transfers work if debt is paid off during 0% intro period
Credit utilization (30% of FICO) should stay below 30%
Payment history (35% of FICO) is the single biggest score factor

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding unsecured debt?
options: Unsecured debt is backed by collateral that the lender can repossess if payments are not made.
Unsecured debt typically carries lower interest rates than secured debt due to the reduced risk for the lender.
Credit card debt is an example of unsecured debt.
Mortgages are a common form of unsecured debt.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Unsecured debt is not backed by collateral, meaning the lender takes on more risk. Credit card debt is a common example of unsecured debt, unlike mortgages which are secured by the property.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens if you fail to pay on a mortgage versus a credit card.

reveal.question: What is a credit score, and why is it important in debt management?
reveal.answer: A credit score is a numerical representation of your creditworthiness, based on your credit history. It's used by lenders to assess the risk of lending you money. A higher credit score typically results in lower interest rates and better terms on loans and credit cards, making debt management easier and cheaper.

rows.label: Definition
values: Moving debt from one or more credit cards to a new credit card, usually with a lower interest rate.
Combining multiple debts into a single new loan or credit account.

rows.label: Number of Accounts
values: Typically involves opening one new credit card account.
May involve opening a new loan or credit account to pay off multiple existing debts.

rows.label: Impact on Credit Score
values: May temporarily lower credit score due to new account and increased credit utilization on existing cards, but can improve it long-term with responsible use.
May temporarily lower credit score due to new account, but can improve it long-term with consistent payments and simplified debt management.

title: Emergency Funds and Liquidity Planning
description: Calculate appropriate emergency fund amounts and recommend liquidity strategies for different client situations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: An adequate emergency fund is the **foundation** of financial security. Without it, any financial plan is vulnerable to unexpected expenses derailing long-term goals.

sections.title: Purpose of Emergency Funds
sections.content:
Emergency funds cover unexpected expenses and income disruption without:

- Liquidating investments (potentially at a loss)
- Taking on high-interest debt
- Derailing long-term financial plans

sections.title: Common Emergency Uses
sections.content:
- Job loss / income reduction
- Medical expenses
- Major home repairs
- Car repairs / replacement
- Unplanned travel (family emergency)

sections.title: How Much Is Enough?
sections.content: The appropriate emergency fund size depends on income stability and personal circumstances.

sections.title: 📊 Calculating the Target
sections.content:
**Formula:** Monthly Essential Expenses × Months Needed

**Essential Expenses Include:**
- Housing (mortgage/rent, utilities, insurance)
- Food
- Transportation
- Healthcare premiums/costs
- Minimum debt payments
- Insurance premiums

**Do NOT Include:**
- Discretionary spending
- Investment contributions
- Gifts/entertainment
- Non-critical subscriptions

sections.title: 📊 Example Calculation
sections.content:
**Client monthly essentials:**
- Housing: $2,500
- Food: $600
- Transportation: $400
- Healthcare: $300
- Insurance: $200
- Minimum debts: $500
- **Total:** $4,500/month

**Single income earner → 6 months:** $4,500 × 6 = **$27,000 target**

sections.title: Where to Keep Emergency Funds
sections.content: The ideal vehicle balances safety, liquidity, and yield.

sections.title: Secondary Options
rows: Must qualify, uses home as collateral

sections.title: ⚠️ What to Avoid for Emergency Funds
sections.content:
**Never use these for emergency funds:**
- Stocks/mutual funds (market risk)
- CDs with penalties (liquidity issue)
- Physical cash (no growth, theft risk)
- Investments with long settlement

sections.title: Tiered Emergency Fund Strategy
sections.content: For larger emergency funds, consider a tiered approach to optimize both liquidity and yield.

sections.title: Three-Tier Structure

content.definition: High-yield savings account, checking account buffer
Money market funds, I-Bonds (after 1-year holding period)
Short-term Treasury funds, Roth IRA contributions (as last resort)

items: **District Courts**: Trial courts; only forum requiring payment of tax first (refund suit).
**Court of Appeals**: Circuit courts; hear appeals from District and Tax Courts.
**Supreme Court**: Highest court; rarely hears tax cases (writ of certiorari).

sections.title: Special Situations
sections.content: Different client situations require different emergency fund approaches.

sections.title: Dual-Income Couples
sections.content:
May need less if:
- Both jobs are stable
- Either income covers essential expenses
- Good disability insurance

Consider reducing to 3-4 months if other safety nets exist.

sections.title: High-Net-Worth Clients
sections.content:
May satisfy emergency reserve through:
- Taxable brokerage (margin availability)
- Cash value life insurance loans
- HELOC established but unused

sections.title: Retirees
sections.content:
Need larger cash reserves because:
- No earned income to rebuild
- Sequence of returns risk
- Healthcare costs unpredictable

**Recommendation:** 1-2 years in cash/short-term bonds.

sections.title: Building the Fund
sections.content: Multiple strategies can help clients build their emergency fund more quickly.

sections.title: Funding Strategies
rows: Set up recurring transfers to savings
Direct lump sums to emergency fund

sections.title: Priority Order
sections.content:
1. **One month's expenses first** (minimum safety net)
2. **Then full target** (3-6+ months)
3. **Only then** focus on other goals

sections.title: ⚠️ 401(k) Match Exception
sections.content: Always capture employer 401(k) match even while building emergency fund. Free money shouldn't be left on the table.

sections.title: Balancing Competing Priorities
sections.content:
**Common Question:** "Should I pay debt or build emergency fund?"

**Answer:** Do both strategically.

sections.title: Debt vs. Emergency Fund Priority Order
sections.content:
1. Build **$1,000-$2,000** immediate cushion first
2. Pay any extremely high-interest debt (>20%)
3. Continue building toward **1 month's expenses**
4. Resume debt payoff (avalanche method)
5. Complete **full emergency target** (3-6 months)

sections.title: 📊 Key Formula
sections.content:
$$\text{Emergency Fund Target} = \text{Monthly Essential Expenses} \times \text{Months Needed}$$

- **Single income/variable:** 6+ months
- **Dual stable income:** 3 months minimum

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Emergency fund = 3-6 months of essential expenses (more for volatility)
High-yield savings accounts: Best balance of safety, liquidity, and yield
Calculate on essentials only: housing, food, transport, healthcare, debt minimums
Build $1,000 minimum cushion first, then complete full target
Tiered approach: Immediate access (1-2 mo) + near-term (2-4 mo) + extended

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the LEAST appropriate action to take when faced with an unexpected $5,000 expense, assuming all options are available?
options: Withdraw $5,000 from a high-yield savings account designated as an emergency fund.
Take out a $5,000 cash advance on a credit card with an 18% APR.
Liquidate $5,000 worth of shares from a taxable brokerage account, incurring a small capital gain.
Borrow $5,000 from a home equity line of credit (HELOC) at a 6% interest rate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While all options provide access to funds, taking a cash advance on a credit card with a high APR is the least appropriate due to the significant interest costs that will accrue, especially if the balance is not paid off quickly. It defeats the purpose of avoiding high-interest debt.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the long-term cost associated with each option.

reveal.question: List at least three common situations where using an emergency fund would be appropriate.
reveal.answer: Acceptable answers include: Job loss/income reduction, medical expenses, major home repairs, car repairs/replacement, unplanned travel (family emergency), and other unexpected and necessary expenses.

knowledgeCheck.question: A client has a stable job and $10,000 in short-term savings. They are considering investing $8,000 of that savings into a certificate of deposit (CD) with a 12-month term to earn a higher interest rate. What is the PRIMARY risk they should consider?
options: The risk of losing principal if the bank fails.
The risk of inflation eroding the purchasing power of the savings.
The risk of not having enough readily available cash for unexpected expenses.
The risk of missing out on potential gains in the stock market.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The primary risk is reduced liquidity. Tying up a significant portion of their savings in a CD means they may not have enough readily available cash to cover unexpected expenses that might arise during the 12-month term. While other options present valid concerns, this is the most immediate liquidity concern.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose of an emergency fund and how a CD impacts access to cash.

title: Financial Calculator Mastery
description: Set up and use HP 10bII+ and TI BA II Plus calculators to solve complex TVM problems

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Calculator proficiency is **critical** for CFP exam success. Mastering these tools ensures you solve problems quickly and accurately. You'll face dozens of TVM problems on exam day.

sections.title: Approved CFP Exam Calculators
sections.content:
The CFP Board allows ONLY:
- **HP 10bII+** (blue)
- **TI BA II Plus** (or Professional)

You may bring **two calculators** to the exam (recommended: same type, fresh batteries).

sections.title: Initial Setup - HP 10bII+
sections.content: Setting up your calculator correctly before each problem set is essential.

sections.title: HP 10bII+ Essential Settings

content.definition: Default is END (ordinary annuity). To switch: [Orange] [BEG/END]. "BEG" appears when in BEGIN mode

items: **P/YR**: Set Payments Per Year (usually 1 or 12). Press [Blue] [1] [P/YR].
**BGN/END**: Set timing of cash flows. [Blue] [BEG/END].
**Decimals**: Set display decimals. [Disp] [4] (recommended).

sections.title: Initial Setup - TI BA II Plus
sections.content: The TI requires a few more steps to configure properly.

sections.title: TI BA II Plus Essential Settings

content.definition: Press: [2nd] [P/Y], Enter: 1 (annual) or 12 (monthly), Press: [ENTER], then [CE/C] to exit
Press: [2nd] [BGN]. If it shows "BGN", press [2nd] [SET] to toggle to END. Press [CE/C] to exit

items: **P/Y**: [2nd] [P/Y] [1] [Enter] (Set to 1 usually).
**BGN/END**: [2nd] [BGN] [2nd] [SET].
**Format**: [2nd] [Format] set DEC = 4.

sections.title: ⚠️ The Sign Convention (Critical!)
sections.content:
This is the #1 source of calculator errors!

| Cash Direction | Sign |
|----------------|------|
| **Cash going OUT** (you pay) | **NEGATIVE (-)** |
| **Cash coming IN** (you receive) | **POSITIVE (+)** |

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sign Convention
sections.content: **OUT is Negative, IN is Positive** — If money leaves your pocket, it's negative.

sections.title: Basic TVM Problem Types
sections.content: Master these five fundamental TVM problem types.

sections.title: 📊 Type 1: Future Value of a Lump Sum
📊 Type 2: Present Value of an Annuity
sections.content:
**Problem:** What should you pay today for $1,000/month for 10 years at 5%?

| Key | Input |
|-----|-------|
| N | 120 (10 × 12) |
| I/Y | 0.4167 (5 ÷ 12) |
| PMT | 1,000 |
| FV | 0 |
| **Solve PV** | **-94,281.12** |

sections.title: 📊 Type 5: Solving for Time
sections.content:
**Problem:** $500/month at 8% to reach $200,000. How many months?

| Key | Input |
|-----|-------|
| I/Y | 0.6667 (8 ÷ 12) |
| PV | 0 |
| PMT | -500 |
| FV | 200,000 |
| **Solve N** | **199.27 months** |

sections.title: Uneven Cash Flows (NPV/IRR)
sections.content: Many real-world investments have uneven cash flows. Both calculators have dedicated functions for NPV and IRR.

sections.title: HP 10bII+ Cash Flow Entry
sections.content:
1. **Clear:** [Orange] [C ALL]
2. **Enter CF0:** amount [CFj]
3. **Enter CF1:** amount [CFj]
4. Repeat for each cash flow
5. **For NPV:** rate [I/YR], then [Orange] [NPV]
6. **For IRR:** [Orange] [IRR/YR]

sections.title: TI BA II Plus Cash Flow Entry
sections.content:
1. **Enter CF Mode:** [CF]
2. **Clear:** [2nd] [CLR Work]
3. **CF0:** enter value, [ENTER], [↓]
4. **C01:** enter value, [ENTER], [↓]
5. **F01** (frequency): usually 1, [ENTER], [↓]
6. Continue for all cash flows
7. **For NPV:** [NPV], enter I, [ENTER], [↓], [CPT]
8. **For IRR:** [IRR], [CPT]

sections.title: 📊 NPV Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Investment:** -$100,000 today
**Cash flows:** Year 1 = $30,000, Year 2 = $40,000, Year 3 = $50,000
**Required return:** 10%

| Entry | Value |
|-------|-------|
| CF0 | -100,000 |
| CF1 | 30,000 |
| CF2 | 40,000 |
| CF3 | 50,000 |
| I | 10% |
| **NPV** | **-$2,103.68** |

Negative NPV → Reject the investment

sections.title: Common Exam Pitfalls
sections.content: Avoid these frequent calculator mistakes on exam day.

sections.title: ⚠️ Pitfall 1: Wrong P/Y Setting
sections.content:
**Problem:** You set P/Y = 12 for a monthly problem, then forget to change it back.

**Fix:** Always check P/Y before each new problem or set to 1 and adjust I/Y manually.

sections.title: ⚠️ Pitfall 2: Wrong Mode (END vs. BEGIN)
sections.content:
**Problem:** Calculating lease in END mode instead of BEGIN.

**Fix:** Read the problem carefully for "beginning" or "end" of period.

sections.title: ⚠️ Pitfall 3: Forgetting the Sign Convention
sections.content:
**Problem:** All positive numbers give nonsensical answers.

**Fix:** Cash out = negative; Cash in = positive.

sections.title: ⚠️ Pitfall 4: Not Clearing Previous Values
sections.content:
**Problem:** Old PMT value carries into new problem.

**Fix:** Clear TVM memory between problems.

sections.title: ⚠️ Pitfall 5: Annual vs. Monthly Confusion
sections.content:
**Problem:** Using annual rate with monthly periods.

**Fix:** Always match:
- Monthly payments → Monthly rate (annual ÷ 12)
- Annual payments → Annual rate

sections.title: Serial (Two-Stage) Problem Approach
sections.content: Many CFP exam problems involve two stages (e.g., accumulation then distribution). Work backwards from the goal.

sections.title: 📊 Serial Problem Example: Retirement Funding
sections.content:
**Stage 1** (Accumulation): Save $X/year at 8% for 25 years.
**Stage 2** (Distribution): Need $50,000/year for 20 years at 5%.

**Solve Backwards:**

**Step 1:** How much needed at retirement?
- N = 20, I/Y = 5, PMT = 50,000, FV = 0
- **PV = $623,110.52**

**Step 2:** How much to save annually?
- N = 25, I/Y = 8, PV = 0, FV = 623,110.52
- **PMT = $8,527.35**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: TVM Variables
sections.content: **N**ever **I**nvest **P**oor **M**oney **F**ast → **N**, **I**/Y, **P**V, P**M**T, **F**V

sections.title: 📊 Key Formulas
sections.content:
**5 TVM Variables:** N, I/Y, PV, PMT, FV

**Monthly rate:** $\text{Monthly Rate} = \frac{\text{Annual Rate}}{12}$

**Monthly periods:** $\text{Monthly Periods} = \text{Years} \times 12$

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CFP exam allows HP 10bII+ and TI BA II Plus only
Clear TVM memory between problems; check P/Y and BEGIN/END mode
Sign convention: OUTFLOWS = negative, INFLOWS = positive
Match time periods: monthly payments require monthly rate (annual ÷ 12)
Serial problems: Work backwards from future need to today's requirement
Know your calculator cold - practice until it's automatic

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following calculators is NOT explicitly approved for use during the CFP® Certification Examination?
options: HP 10bII+
TI BA II Plus Professional
TI-84 Plus CE
TI BA II Plus
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The CFP Board explicitly approves the HP 10bII+ and both versions of the TI BA II Plus. The TI-84 Plus CE is not on the approved list and therefore prohibited.

reveal.question: How do you clear all memory on the HP 10bII+?
reveal.answer: Press and hold the 'ON' button, then press the 'CLEAR ALL' button while still holding 'ON'. This will reset all registers to zero.

knowledgeCheck.question: Why is it crucial to reset and configure your financial calculator before starting each new problem set on the CFP® exam?
options: To ensure the calculator functions correctly.
To prevent carrying over incorrect values or settings from previous calculations.
To comply with CFP Board regulations regarding calculator usage.
Both B and C
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While having a functioning calculator and following regulations are important, the primary reason to reset is to eliminate any lingering values or settings from prior calculations that could lead to errors in the current problem.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the impact of previous calculations on your current problem.

reveal.question: What does the 'P/YR' setting represent on a financial calculator, and why is it important to set it correctly?
reveal.answer: The 'P/YR' setting represents the number of payments per year. Setting it correctly is crucial because it directly impacts the calculated interest rates and periodic payments, leading to inaccurate results if set incorrectly. Common values are 1 (annual), 4 (quarterly), 12 (monthly), and 26 (bi-weekly).
